items.q: Which energy system fuels a marathon?
items.why: Students associate hard exercise with anaerobic systems. Long-duration steady-state work uses AEROBIC metabolism (oxygen + carbs/fats) - efficient but slower to ramp up. Anaerobic systems dominate brief intense efforts.

items.correct: Volume of air inhaled or exhaled in one normal breath at rest.
items.misconception: The maximum air your lungs can hold.
items.random_wrong: Air left in the lungs after maximum exhalation.
items.why: Students confuse tidal volume (resting breath, ~0.5L) with vital capacity (max breath, ~4-5L). Different lung volumes serve different purposes - tidal is the normal small ebb and flow.

items.q: What is the rough formula for maximum heart rate?
items.why: Students misremember the formula. 220 - age is the classic estimate (rough; varies individually). A 20-year-old's estimated max HR is around 200 bpm; an 80-year-old's is around 140.
The answer examiners want is: Cranium. “Ribs” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Where two bones meet. “A muscle” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly how many bones does an adult have?
items.why: The real figure is 206. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
